template:
**DOCUMENT FORMAT — SURVEILLANCE / VIDEO PRESERVATION LETTER (PI — PREMISES)**:
An urgent preservation-of-evidence letter directed at the operator of a premises (retailer, restaurant, hospitality property, parking operator, apartment complex, school, government facility) where the subject incident occurred — and at any third-party that operates surveillance on or near the premises. Critical timing instrument: most commercial CCTV systems retain recordings for **only 7 to 30 days** before automatic overwrite, and digital-video recorder (DVR) auto-purge cannot be reversed. Target length: 2–3 pages.

**WHAT THIS TEMPLATE IS — AND WHAT IT IS NOT**:
This is a video-surveillance-specific extension of the generic preservation letter. It supplements (does NOT replace) `preservation_letter_pi` — the generic letter goes to defendants, parties, and corporate-records custodians; this letter goes specifically to whoever controls the cameras and DVR/NVR systems and the third-party security integrator if applicable. It is NOT a Rule 34 discovery request — that is filed after suit and addressed to a party. This letter is pre-suit and is grounded in the common-law and Sedona-Commentary duty to preserve.

**DYNAMIC BEHAVIOR — READ documentOptions**:
- `recipientType`: `premises_owner` (default) | `property_manager` | `retailer` | `restaurant_or_hospitality` | `parking_operator` | `apartment_complex` | `school` | `government_facility` | `security_integrator_third_party` | `adjacent_business`
- `subPractice`: `premises` (default) | `mva` (parking-lot collisions; intersection-adjacent business cameras) | `workers_comp_third_party`
- `preservationWindow`: `incident_day_only` | `window_24h_around` (default) | `window_72h_around` | `extended_3_to_7_days` | `extended_full_week`
- `cameraScope`: array — `interior_floor_cameras`, `exterior_perimeter`, `parking_lot_and_drive`, `entry_and_exit`, `loading_dock`, `cashier_or_pos_cameras`, `restroom_corridor`, `stairwell`, `elevator_interior`, `customer_service_desk`
- `includeAuditTrailRequest`: boolean (default true) — request audit trail/metadata for any pull/edit/redaction
- `includePOSDataRequest`: boolean (default false; retail-relevant) — synchronize timestamped point-of-sale data with video
- `includeIncidentReportRequest`: boolean (default true)
- `includeWitnessStatementRequest`: boolean (default true)
- `includePhotographRequest`: boolean (default true)
- `includeMaintenanceLogRequest`: boolean (default true; mode-of-operation cases)
- `includeBodyCameraOrThirdPartyCameraRequest`: boolean (default true) — adjacent businesses, doorbell cameras, government PTZ cameras

**STRUCTURE** (follow line by line):

1. Date line.
2. VIA line (bold) — `**VIA Certified Mail — Return Receipt Requested AND Email AND Hand Delivery**` (default; surveillance overwrite cycles make this letter critically time-sensitive — same delivery posture as vehicle preservation letters).
3. Recipient block — facility name; manager / risk manager / general counsel; address.
4. Re: block (multi-line, aligned labels):
   Re:    Our Client:           [client name]
          Date of Incident:     [date of incident]
          Time of Incident:     [time of incident, if known]
          Location of Incident: [premises address; specific area within]
          Subject:              **URGENT — Notice of Anticipated Litigation; Demand for Preservation of Surveillance Video and Related Records**
5. Greeting — `Dear [Recipient name]:` if known, else `Dear Risk Manager:` for institutional recipients.

**BODY SECTIONS** (in order):

### I. Notice of Representation and Urgency
One paragraph: this office represents the client identified above. Our client sustained personal injuries on [date of incident] at [time, if known] at the location identified above. Surveillance video, related contemporaneous records, and physical conditions of the premises are critical evidence. Litigation is reasonably anticipated. State explicitly: 'This letter is URGENT. Most commercial surveillance systems automatically overwrite recordings within seven (7) to thirty (30) days. The recipient is on notice that any surveillance footage covering the date, time, and location of the incident — and the categories below — must be preserved IMMEDIATELY in native format and segregated from the auto-overwrite cycle.'

### II. The Duty to Preserve
One paragraph citing:
- Common-law preservation duty under **Silvestri v. General Motors Corp., 271 F.3d 583 (4th Cir. 2001)**.
- Federal ESI preservation under **Fed. R. Civ. P. 37(e)** — surveillance video is electronically stored information squarely within the rule.
- Litigation-hold issuance per **Zubulake v. UBS Warburg LLC, 229 F.R.D. 422 (S.D.N.Y. 2004)** ('Zubulake V') and **ABA Civil Discovery Standards Standard 10**.
- The Sedona Conference Commentary on Legal Holds, 11 Sedona Conf. J. 265 (2010).
- For premises matters, the **mode-of-operation** doctrine (where applicable per JURISDICTION CONTEXT — e.g., **Owens v. Publix Super Markets, Inc., 802 So. 2d 315 (Fla. 2001)** [recodified by Fla. Stat. § 768.0755], or analogous foreign-substance-on-floor frameworks) makes contemporaneous video and inspection-log evidence dispositive.

### III. Scope of Preservation — Surveillance Video
One paragraph: preserve and segregate from auto-overwrite — in **native, unedited file format (no conversion, redaction, compression, watermarking, or annotation)** — surveillance video covering:
- The incident itself.
- The **window before and after the incident**: per `preservationWindow` (default ± 24 hours from the incident timestamp; mode-of-operation cases typically require 24–72 hours pre-incident to demonstrate the dangerous condition's duration; `extended_full_week` for slip-and-fall under heavy-traffic conditions).
- The **camera locations** in `cameraScope`. At a minimum: any camera with line of sight to the incident location; exterior perimeter cameras facing the relevant entrance/exit; parking-lot and drive cameras; loading-dock cameras (mode-of-operation cases); cashier/POS cameras (sync with timestamped sales records).
State explicitly that preservation must include the **complete audit trail** for the recordings — timestamp metadata, camera identifier, frame rate, resolution, and any pull/edit/redaction history.

### IV. Scope of Preservation — Audit Trail and Metadata [include only when includeAuditTrailRequest=true]
One paragraph: preserve and produce the audit log of any access to, copy from, edit of, or redaction of the surveillance recordings — including the identity of the user(s), date/time of access, the action taken, and the device used. Preserve the original recording in addition to any working copy. Do NOT delete the source after copying.

### V. Scope of Preservation — Synchronized Records
One paragraph: preserve the contemporaneous records that contextualize the video — including:
- **Incident reports** [when includeIncidentReportRequest=true] — the original incident or accident report prepared by store/facility personnel; supplemental reports; manager's report; risk-management notification.
- **Witness statements** [when includeWitnessStatementRequest=true] — written, recorded, or transcribed statements from employees, customers, and bystanders.
- **Photographs** [when includePhotographRequest=true] — any photographs taken by employees, security, or risk-management personnel of the scene, the dangerous condition, the client's injuries, or the cleanup.
- **Point-of-sale data** [when includePOSDataRequest=true] — timestamped POS transactions, customer counts, and traffic-flow data for the relevant time window, to corroborate video timestamps and traffic conditions.
- **Inspection / maintenance logs** [when includeMaintenanceLogRequest=true] — sweep logs, inspection logs, mopping logs, spill-response logs, and maintenance work orders for the area in the 72 hours before the incident (mode-of-operation foundational evidence).
- **Personnel records** — schedules, time clocks, and assignment records for employees on duty in the relevant area at the relevant time.
- **Training and policy records** — relevant safety-policy manuals, sweep / inspection / spill-response procedures, and training records for employees on duty.

### VI. Third-Party and Adjacent Cameras [include only when includeBodyCameraOrThirdPartyCameraRequest=true]
One paragraph: please identify and provide preservation requests to any third party that operates a camera with potential line of sight to the incident — including:
- Adjacent businesses (neighboring tenants, parking-garage operators, ATMs).
- Government cameras (municipal CCTV, traffic cameras, transit-authority cameras).
- Security-integrator vendors that maintain the system on the recipient's behalf (typically the holder of the actual video).
- Doorbell cameras (Ring, Nest) of nearby residences (where applicable).
This office reserves the right to subpoena or follow up directly with each third party.

### VII. No Alteration or Disclosure
One paragraph: do NOT redact, edit, watermark, downsample, transcode, or otherwise alter the recordings. Do NOT release the recordings to any third party — including media, opposing counsel, or the recipient's insurer — without first providing this office written notice and an opportunity to object. Any production should preserve the original native file in addition to any annotated working copy.

### VIII. Sanctions for Spoliation
One paragraph: this letter places the recipient on notice that any destruction, overwriting, or unauthorized release of surveillance footage exposes the recipient to:
- Adverse-inference jury instruction.
- Monetary sanctions, attorneys' fees, and costs.
- Preclusion of evidence.
- Under Fed. R. Civ. P. 37(e), where intentional spoliation is found, dispositive sanctions including default judgment and dismissal.
- In some jurisdictions, an independent tort cause of action for spoliation. State explicitly: 'This notice eliminates inadvertence as a defense, and a routine auto-overwrite cycle that runs after this letter is received will not insulate the recipient.'

### IX. Confirmation Request and Closing
One paragraph: please provide written confirmation within **seven (7) days** that (1) the surveillance recordings covering the date, time, location, and `preservationWindow` have been preserved on segregated media, (2) the auto-overwrite cycle has been suspended for the preserved recordings, (3) the synchronized records (incident reports, witness statements, photographs, POS data, sweep logs) have been collected and segregated, and (4) the security-integrator vendor has been placed on notice. Provide the contact information for the system administrator. Direct all communications to undersigned counsel.

Closing: `Very truly yours,` (suppress when stationery signature block is applied).

**CONSTRAINTS**:
- Do NOT cite a specific state mode-of-operation framework, sweep/inspection statute, or spoliation tort unless JURISDICTION CONTEXT supplies it. (E.g., Fla. Stat. § 768.0755 has effectively recodified Owens v. Publix in Florida; states differ widely. Defer to JURISDICTION CONTEXT.)
- Do NOT promise the recipient anonymity or confidentiality — preservation letters are routinely produced as exhibits to spoliation motions.
- For any disabled `documentOptions` value, suppress the corresponding section entirely — never emit a placeholder-only line.
- If firm stationery is applied, SUPPRESS the `Very truly yours,` closing and signature block.
- Total body text: 700–1,400 words. Target page count: 2–3 pages.
hints: Surveillance / video preservation letter — premises-specific extension of preservation_letter_pi. Time-critical: most commercial CCTV systems auto-overwrite every 7-30 days. Cite Silvestri (4th Cir. 2001), FRCP 37(e), Zubulake V, ABA Civ. Discovery Std. 10, Sedona Commentary on Legal Holds. For premises mode-of-operation cases, defer to JURISDICTION CONTEXT (Owens v. Publix in FL recodified by § 768.0755; varies widely by state). Preserve native format — no transcoding, redaction, or watermarking. Include synchronized records (incident reports, witness statements, photographs, POS data, sweep/inspection logs) for mode-of-operation foundation. 2–3 pages.

verificationSources.citation: Silvestri v. General Motors Corp., 271 F.3d 583 (4th Cir. 2001)
Fed. R. Civ. P. 37(e) (Failure to Preserve Electronically Stored Information)
Zubulake v. UBS Warburg LLC, 229 F.R.D. 422 (S.D.N.Y. 2004) (Zubulake V)
ABA Civil Discovery Standards Standard 10 (Litigation Hold)
The Sedona Conference Commentary on Legal Holds: The Trigger and the Process, 11 Sedona Conf. J. 265 (2010)
Owens v. Publix Super Markets, Inc., 802 So. 2d 315 (Fla. 2001) (mode-of-operation reference; Florida-specific framework subsequently recodified)

knownLimitations: Surveillance overwrite cycles vary widely — small retail (7 days), franchise hospitality (14 days), large-format retail and corporate hospitality (30 days), enterprise security integrators (30–90 days). Confirm the system specifications when scheduling follow-up.

The actual holder of the video is often the third-party security integrator, NOT the on-site facility manager. A separate hold to the integrator may be required (use `recipientType=security_integrator_third_party`).

Adjacent-business and doorbell-camera footage requires coordination with non-defendants — these are typically cooperative but require their own preservation requests; subpoena follow-up may be needed.
Government CCTV (municipal, transit, traffic) is governed by FOIA / state public-records statutes — separate request workflow.

State mode-of-operation frameworks vary: Florida recodified Owens via Fla. Stat. § 768.0755 to require knowledge; New Jersey continues a more plaintiff-friendly framework under Nisivoccia v. Glass Gardens. Defer to JURISDICTION CONTEXT.
Hospital and medical-facility surveillance is often subject to HIPAA — production may require redaction of patient identifiers; preservation duty applies but production format may differ.
School and government-facility surveillance may implicate FERPA, state public-records exemptions, or law-enforcement-records exemptions. Coordinate with the public-records officer.
